summary:
Postauricular lymphadenopathy in kids often results from ear infections like chronic suppurative otitis media due to close anatomical proximity between these areas; this makes option D most appropriate based on typical presentations seen clinically for such conditions.
The most prevalent reason for post-ear infection-related swelling behind the ears in young patients stems primarily from ongoing middle ear problems that directly affect surrounding tissues including local lymph nodes, aligning closely with choice D as indicated by observed medical patterns. Agent1 identified "chronic suppurative otitis media" as the leading factor because of how frequently it leads to swollen glands near or around affected ears compared to other potential sources listed. This condition creates an environment conducive to localized inflammatory responses affecting draining lymphatic tissue effectively causing visible enlargement noted during physical examination procedures conducted regularly within pediatric settings addressing suspected cases of enlarged posterior auricular area swellings indicative of possible underlying infectious processes impacting both auditory canal regions along with their corresponding drainage systems represented via superficial cervical chain node clusters observable externally upon inspection. 

In summary:

Chronic suppurative otitis media stands out as the predominant trigger for postauricular lymphadenopathy owing to its propensity towards creating significant inflammatory reactions locally resulting in noticeable glandular enlargements detectable physically – distinguishing it clearly amongst alternative possibilities considered here according to established diagnostic criteria used widely across healthcare practices catering particularly toward managing childhood illnesses characterized notably by symptoms manifesting prominently at head/neck junctions prone sites harboring secondary
